title: Rajma
tagline: Slow-simmered kidney beans in a smooth onion-tomato masala.
servingsNote: Scales up easily — double everything for 4.
macroNotes: Estimated from ingredients (dry rajma weight, before rice).
Solid macros on its own — high fibre and decent plant protein. Served with 1 cup cooked rice, add ~200 kcal and ~4 g protein per serving.
Protein boost: stir in 100 g soaked, squeezed, roughly chopped soya chunks (50 g per serving) with the beans at step 6. Adds ~13 g protein per serving and soaks up the gravy nicely.
For extra protein without changing the dish, finish with a spoon of thick curd per bowl or serve alongside a boiled egg.

items: 1 cup (180 g) dry rajma (red kidney beans), soaked 8 hours or overnight
100 g dry soya chunks (optional, for protein)
3 cups (720 ml) water for pressure cooking
1 raw potato, peeled and halved (only if you oversalt)

steps.title: Soak the beans
steps.body: Soak 1 cup (180 g) dry rajma in plenty of water for 8 hours or overnight. This matters — under-soaked rajma stays hard in the middle no matter how long you cook it. Drain and rinse before cooking.

steps.title: Pressure cook
steps.body: Add the soaked rajma to a pressure cooker with 3 cups (720 ml) water and 1 tsp salt. Cook on high until the first whistle, then lower the heat and cook 15–18 minutes (about 5–6 whistles). The beans should mash easily when pressed between two fingers — that softness is what makes the gravy creamy later.

steps.title: Soak the soya (optional)
steps.body: If using, soak 100 g dry soya chunks in hot (not cold) water for 10 minutes, then squeeze each one dry hard — squeezing out the water is what stops them tasting spongy. Roughly chop and set aside.

steps.title: Temper
steps.body: Heat 2 tbsp oil or ghee in a pan. Add 1 tsp cumin seeds and 1 bay leaf; let the cumin sizzle for 20 seconds.

steps.title: Fry the onions
steps.body: Add 2 finely chopped onions and fry on medium heat until deep golden, 8–10 minutes. Don't rush this — pale onions make a flat rajma. Add 1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste and cook 1 minute until the raw smell goes.

steps.title: Cook the masala
steps.body: Add the puree of 3 tomatoes, 1/2 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp red chilli powder, 1.5 tsp coriander powder, 1/2 tsp garam masala and 3/4 tsp salt. Cook on medium 8–10 minutes until the oil separates at the edges and the masala looks glossy, not raw.

steps.title: Combine
steps.body: Tip in the cooked rajma with its cooking liquid (and the squeezed soya chunks if using). Stir, then mash a few beans against the side of the pan to thicken the gravy naturally. Simmer 12–15 minutes so the beans drink up the masala.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Stir in 1/2 tsp garam masala, 1 tsp crushed kasuri methi and 2 tbsp chopped coriander. Simmer 2 more minutes. Serve hot with rice (rajma-chawal) or roti.

notes: SALT RESCUE: if the rajma comes out too salty, drop in 1–2 pieces of peeled raw potato and simmer 10–15 minutes, then remove them — the potato pulls out a good amount of the excess salt. You can also stretch it with more water, extra tomato puree or a few more cooked beans, or mellow it with a spoon of cream or curd. A pinch of sugar or a squeeze of lemon also masks over-salting.
Always soak the rajma properly — it's the single biggest factor in whether the beans turn creamy.
Mashing a few beans instead of adding cornflour keeps the gravy thick and authentic.
source: Adapted from my own cooking notes.
